content:
# Matthew Chapter 7 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/MAT_007_page_001.jpg, MAT_007_page_002.jpg

### Judging Others

1. "Don't judge others, and you won't be judged.

2. For you'll be judged by the same standard you use, and the measure you give will be the measure you get back.

3. Why do you fixate on the splinter in your brother's eye while ignoring the plank in your own?

4. How can you say to your brother, 'Here, let me get that splinter out of your eye,' when you've got a plank sticking out of yours?

5. Hypocrite! First pull the plank out of your own eye — then you'll see well enough to deal with the splinter in your brother's.

6. Don't give sacred things to dogs. Don't throw your pearls to pigs — they'll trample them in the mud and then turn and attack you.

### Ask, Seek, Knock

7. Ask, and you will receive. Seek, and you will find. Knock, and the door will be opened to you.

8. For everyone who asks receives. Everyone who seeks finds. And to everyone who knocks, the door is opened.

9. Think about it: if your child asks for bread, would you hand him a stone?

10. If he asks for a fish, would you give him a snake?

11. If you, with all your flaws, know how to give good things to your children, how much more will your Father in heaven give good things to those who ask him!

12. So in everything, treat others the way you want to be treated. This sums up the Law and the Prophets.

### The Narrow Gate

13. Enter through the narrow gate. The wide gate and the broad road lead to ruin, and many people take that way.

14. But the narrow gate and the difficult road lead to life — and only a few ever find it.

### A Tree and Its Fruit

15. Watch out for false prophets. They come to you looking like sheep on the outside, but underneath they are vicious wolves.

16. You'll know them by what they produce. Do you pick grapes from thornbushes? Figs from thistles?

17. Every good tree produces good fruit, and every rotten tree produces bad fruit.

18. A good tree cannot produce bad fruit, and a rotten tree cannot produce good fruit.

19. Every tree that fails to produce good fruit is chopped down and thrown into the fire.

20. So yes — you'll know them by what they produce.

### True and False Disciples

21. Not everyone who calls me 'Lord, Lord' will enter the kingdom of heaven — only those who do the will of my Father in heaven.

22. On that day, many will say to me, 'Lord, Lord! Didn't we prophesy in your name? Didn't we drive out demons in your name? Didn't we perform miracles in your name?'

23. And I will tell them plainly: 'I never knew you. Get away from me, you who practice lawlessness.'

### The Wise and Foolish Builders

24. Anyone who hears what I'm saying and puts it into practice is like a wise man who built his house on solid rock.

25. The rain poured down, the rivers rose, the winds howled and hammered that house — but it stood firm, because it was built on rock.

26. But anyone who hears what I'm saying and doesn't act on it is like a fool who built his house on sand.

27. The rain poured down, the rivers rose, the winds howled and slammed into that house — and it collapsed. What a spectacular ruin it was!"

28. When Jesus finished this teaching, the crowds were stunned.

29. He taught with an authority of his own — nothing like the scribes they were used to.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 3 — "splinter/plank" for κάρφος/δοκός:** The Greek uses the contrast between a tiny splinter (κάρφος) and a construction beam (δοκός). The image is deliberately absurd — a person with a wooden beam protruding from their eye offering to perform delicate eye surgery on someone else. The comedy is the point.

**Verse 6 — "sacred things to dogs... pearls to pigs":** These are hard sayings. In the Jewish context, dogs and pigs were unclean animals. The saying warns against offering what is precious to those who will not value it — and who may become hostile. Jesus is being practical, not dismissive.

**Verse 12 — "This sums up the Law and the Prophets":** The positive Golden Rule is Jesus's summary of the entire Torah and prophetic tradition. It is stunningly simple and infinitely demanding.

**Verse 22-23 — the devastating reversal:** The people who are turned away are not outsiders — they are people who called Jesus "Lord" and performed spectacular ministry in his name. The problem isn't lack of activity but lack of relationship ("I never knew you") and lack of obedience to God's will. This is one of the most unsettling passages in the Gospels.

**Verse 27 — "What a spectacular ruin it was!":** The Greek καὶ ἦν ἡ πτῶσις αὐτῆς μεγάλη — literally "and the fall of it was great." The understated Greek is devastating. PNEUMA renders it with an exclamation that captures the finality and scale of the collapse.

**Verse 29 — "an authority of his own":** The Greek contrast is between teaching ὡς ἐξουσίαν ἔχων ("as having authority") and "not as the scribes." Scribes derived their authority from quoting other rabbis. Jesus quoted no one but himself ("But I say to you..."). The crowds recognized something fundamentally different.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
